nativeRange.habitat: Shallow coral reef slopes and sandy-rubble reef zones; typically found anchored between rounded stones on reef flats and lagoon floors with moderate-to-strong water movement

decorPreferences: rounded stones or rubble for anchoring between
open floor space to expand oral disc
ample distance from corals and sessile invertebrates

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via hosted zooxanthellae, supplemented by active predation. Has been known to accept chopped silversides, fresh or frozen shrimp, mussel flesh, and similar meaty foods offered directly to the oral disc. Sources recommend feeding 1–2 times per week; the anemone will also opportunistically capture any small fish that makes contact with its disc.

goodWith: Clark's Clownfish (Amphiprion clarkii) — occasional host relationship
large, robust fish unable to be engulfed
avoidWith: small fish of any species — potent adhesive sting can capture and kill them
most clownfish species other than A. clarkii — will not host and may injure or kill them
other anemone species — will sting competing anemones
corals and sessile invertebrates within reach of disc expansion
delicate or slow-moving invertebrates near the disc

summary: The Pizza Anemone is a rare, visually striking anemone with a distinctively textured radial disc and two tentacle types. It hosts an extremely potent, adhesive sting — advanced care only; generally hostile to fish and most clownfish.
careNotes:
Cryptodendrum adhaesivum is commonly known as the Pizza Anemone or Adhesive Sea Anemone, referring to its distinctive dual-tentacle disc pattern and its famously sticky, toxic tentacles. It is the sole species in genus Cryptodendrum.

Appearance: The oral disc bears two distinct tentacle types. Central tentacles are short (~5 mm), branched with five or more finger-like lobes resembling inflated rubber gloves. Marginal tentacles have bubble-like tips approximately 1 mm in diameter. The two zones typically display different coloration, producing the 'pizza slice' patterning that gives it its popular name. Disc diameter in captivity is generally reported in the 25–30 cm range; wild specimens in optimal conditions have been described as reaching up to 50 cm or larger, though reports of 'over 3 feet' (LiveAquaria) appear to conflate this species with the larger carpet anemones (Stichodactyla spp.).

Stinging potency: This species is considered one of the most potently adhesive anemones kept in the aquarium hobby. The short central tentacles are highly toxic and extremely sticky, capable of firmly grabbing and retaining fish, invertebrates, and even a hobbyist's skin. Any small fish making contact with the disc has been known to be captured and consumed. Exercise caution during tank maintenance — tentacles will adhere to hands and are difficult to remove without damaging the anemone or causing nematocyst discharge. Use gloves and tools when working near this species.

Clownfish hosting: Amphiprion clarkii (Clark's Clownfish) is the confirmed natural symbiont and the only anemonefish reliably documented hosting C. adhaesivum in the wild. Scientific literature treats A. clarkii as the sole known host; most field surveys show specimens without any anemonefish, suggesting many wild anemones go unhosted. Some trade sources also list A. polymnus and A. sebae as potential hosts, but these are not broadly corroborated. Critically: the vast majority of clownfish species — including popular species such as A. ocellaris, A. percula, A. frenatus, and others — will NOT be hosted and are at serious risk of capture and death if they contact the disc. Keepers should not assume any clownfish will be accepted. Even A. clarkii pairings may not form immediately or at all in captivity, and there are documented cases of A. clarkii being injured.

Aggression and tank placement: The Pizza Anemone has been known to roam freely until it finds a preferred location. It should be added to the aquarium before corals and other sessile invertebrates are placed, so it can settle without disturbing established livestock. Once positioned, it may expand its disc to 30–50 cm and will sting or consume anything within reach. A buffer zone of at least 15–20 cm from other corals and invertebrates is strongly advised. Its tentacles may sting and damage soft corals and LPS placed nearby.

Water parameters: Cross-source temperatures ranged from 22°C (LiveAquaria lower bound) to 29.1°C (Reeflex upper bound). The adopted range of 24–27°C represents an average of three sources and aligns with typical high-quality reef conditions. Salinity sources gave 1.020–1.025 (LiveAquaria) and 1.023–1.025 (Reeflings); the averaged range of 1.021–1.025 is used here.

Minimum tank: Sources ranged from 114 L (30 gal, LiveAquaria) to 300 L (Reeflex). Given the anemone's roaming behavior, large adult size, and the need for substantial buffer zones around it, 200 L is adopted as a cautious minimum for a specimen that has settled; larger tanks are preferred.

Care difficulty: Despite moderate ratings from some retail sources, the consensus from specialist keepers is that this species is challenging to maintain long-term. It has a reputation for declining in captivity without stable, high-quality water and consistent feeding. It is not recommended for beginners or intermediate keepers.

Feeding: Offer meaty foods (shrimp, silversides, mussel) 1–2 times per week by pipette or tongs — never fingers. The zooxanthellae supplement nutrition via photosynthesis and require high lighting; reduce feeding frequency only when lighting is verified adequate (150+ PAR) and the anemone appears fully inflated and healthy.

Reproduction: Captive reproduction via pedal laceration (fission) has been observed; sexual reproduction occurs in the wild. Not deliberately bred in the hobby.

Associated commensals: In addition to A. clarkii, the species is documented hosting porcelain crabs (Neopetrolisthes maculatus), Periclimenes shrimp spp., and Thor amboinensis (sexy shrimp) in commensal (not mutualistic) relationships. These associations appear to be tolerated rather than beneficial to the anemone.
breedingNotes: Not deliberately bred in the aquarium hobby. Pedal laceration (asexual fission) has been observed in captivity. Sexual reproduction occurs via spawning in the wild. No reliable captive breeding protocols are documented.

additional.name: Reeflings — Pizza Anemone Complete Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (25–28°C), salinity (1.023–1.025), pH, tank size (200 L), care level (moderate-easy), and clownfish compatibility. Temperature upper bound adopted from this source in the average. Tank size of 200 L used as cautious midpoint between this source (200 L) and Reeflex (300 L).

additional.name: Reeflex — Cryptodendrum adhaesivum
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (24.7–29.1°C), adult disc size (25–30 cm), depth range (0–49 m), care level (advanced only), and minimum tank size (300 L). Depth range adopted from this source. Care level 'advanced only' supported by specialist consensus. Tank size upper bound noted but 200 L used as practical minimum.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Cryptodendrum adhaesivum
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (Family Thalassianthidae, Order Actiniaria), clownfish host confirmation (A. clarkii as sole documented host), associated commensals (Neopetrolisthes maculatus, Periclimenes spp., Thor amboinensis, Dascyllus trimaculatus), and morphology notes. Confirms most specimens found without anemonefish partners.

additional.name: Lizard Island Field Guide — Adhesive Sea Anemone
additional.notes: Scientific field reference confirming A. clarkii as sole known anemonefish associate; documents that specimens at Lizard Island were found without anemonefish. Used to corroborate restricted clownfish hosting claim.
